author: @dudleysmith5111
text: Why not let the water wheel (or wind turbine or solar panel) generate electricity and utilize that power to either heat or chill a large mass of water in an insulated container. Then one would only need small amounts on electricity for pumping the heater/chilled water through heat exchangers to utilize the heat/cold to control the temperature in a home. I understand the McDonnell-Douglas helicopter plant in Mesa, Arizona (a very hot climate), buys the major part of their electricity at night when the demand is down and the prices significantly lower to chill water in tank the size of 3 football fields buried underground and then air conditions their facilities during the day using only heat exchangers. The fluid is pumped through the tank to the heat exchangers and low power fans blow air across the heat exchangers and circulate chilled air through the facility. In this example, water is used to store energy. One could possibly use two tanks, one with heated water and one with chilled water to uses as sources to drive a Stirling generator for electric use later. There are many  possibilities.

author: @crypticnomad
text: I think water is a solution to our collective energy problems. One of the things I've been looking at is combining the phenomenon of water hammer with a fluidic amplifier like in the patent US4000757. The basic idea is to have a large chamber full of water acting as a source for head and flow into a smaller/lower chamber, use the force generated when turning off the water and the fluidic amplifier I mentioned(which has 10-15x gains) to pump the water back into the large container. Each step has losses but the use of the water hammer combined with the amplifier should be more than enough to move the water back uphill and especially if it is done in a creative way like with a piping system modeled after a 3d hilbert curve. This wouldn't generate electricity but rather generate work in the form of compressed air, increased water flow speed and being able to move water uphill.

replies.author: @simonmasters3295
replies.text: Diagram please? You lost me before Hilbert curve

replies.author: @crypticnomad
replies.text: @simonmasters3295 So that patent I mentioned shows a fluidic amplifier that has a gain of about 10-15x and if you lookup the wikipedia page on "fluidics" they cover that specific patent about halfway down the page. To be clear I'm not saying it is over unity or whatever but rather that it can take either water flow or pressure as an input and output 10-15x of the opposite. Meaning if one wanted to target pressure then they would input water and the pressure output would be 10-15x the input pressure and vice versa for water flow. Water hammer is kind of analogous to flyback voltage and comes from turning off water too quickly. Depending on the head of pressure it can send a rather huge spike of pressure back through the system. In old houses with poor plumbing we could hear it as popping sounds as the pipes physically moved from the pressure after turning off the valve.

author: @World_Theory
text:
I had heard of British homes having water tanks in the attic, but the descrition of those I heard was of an open tank. Is that different from an accumulator?

Another thing: I hadn't heard of accumulators, being a common fixture in British home. They do sound nice for energy storage. I actually think it would be better to directly transmit and store mechanical energy in an accumulator from something like a windmill. Might need to do something to make a windmill capable of lifting a heavy plunger. Especially if you use a very large accumulator. Ideally, the transformation system should be long lasting, low maintenance, reasonably cheap, efficient, and compact enough not to dillute the energy density of the accumulator to the point of uselessness.

I like flywheels too. They're neat. But would like the idea of using them to store wing power too. But how do you dynamically gear up the output of a windmill to contribute speed to a flywheel as the flywheel gradually goes up in speed?

author: @martinsmallridge4025
text:
One key limitation to this idea… Weight.

In a domestic scenario your house will only be able to hold a limited amount of water in a tank before it stresses your house structure. Additionally there’s the need to keep it above freezing and insulation to stop it drawing off too much of your houses hear during colder months.

So, some practical issues to address but still interesting idea

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: Tanks buried underground outside would help with both of these issues. Temps stabilized by the thermal mass inertia of the ground. Insulated towards the top.

author: @tarasmostyka2027
text: If we store energy in a pressurized water tank, and water is not combustable liquid... Then the energy is stored not in the water, but in something that makes pressure in accumulator (air, weight etc.), right? So the actual power capacity will be limited by the nature of this agent and geometry of accumulator. I belive this is the reason for such systems are not used in in-house appliances.

author: @Jarvis30
text:
The question isn't if its possible, its if its practical.
What is the efficiency? And what is the total usable energy you could pull from a single residential version of this.  
I'm guessing not very much for the effort and maintenance.   
It would be much simpler to use a weight based system as in clocks and not worry about water freezing.
The energy has to get into the system somehow and the only reason to do it is because the grid may go down.  So I would concentrate on the easiest way to create the energy to store and use when needed and with the best efficiency. 
Now i live on 3 Acres that is long and sloped so i could easily have 10s of thousands of gallons of water in an artificial pond at one end and harvest the water energy flowing to a lower pond, then use wind & solar electricity to pump the water back to the higher pond. 
Unfortunately most people do not have that setup.
With weights in a single resistance you could have a car, horse, or a few people lift a large weight with a pulley system. Even a simple 5 ton Chainfall system with a solar motor hoisting when the sun is out would be awesome.
I just don't think water systems at small scale will provide a useful amount of energy.
I like it.  Would love to have an accumulator instead of a powerwall.  Use it as a battery for my solar to provide power overnight.

replies.author: @AORD72
replies.text:
F=mgh. If you have 1 cubic meter of water (1 tonne) at 4 meters high, and 50% efficiency you could make 5 Watts (useless). If your power costs $0.30 per kWh, you would save yourself $0.0015  (useless). Hardly any energy stored in mass at height. This is why people learn mathematics, so they can easily determine the effectiveness of ideas before wasting time making them. 
Robert sensationalises known ideas with the English language, however if you use the language of the universe (mathematics) you can learn the reality.

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text: @AORD72 , I think you may need to revisit your maths class, jk... Potential Energy(PE) is = m*g*h (force times height or displacement). Power is the time derivative of Energy. How fast one uses the stored energy governs the power one gets (more power delivered, consumes the energy quicker). Yes lifting weights produces dismally low return on investment. 1 kWh, is equivalent to 3.6 MegaJoules.  If lifting water, 10 cubic metres,  lifted 37 metres high (against the gravitational force) is equivalent to 1kWh (double the; mass, height or gravity to allow for the 50% efficiency) yep all for pennies.

replies.author: @AORD72
replies.text: @kadmow My bad I, meant to put E not F, and I should have typed 5 Wh ,the 5 Wh is still correct (useless). But you know what I meant, I doubt 90% of people that watch Roberts videos would understand the physics or maths of it.
